title: Scaling Graph Chain-of-Thought Reasoning: A Multi-Agent Framework with Efficient LLM Serving
core_contribution: GLM is the first multi-agent Graph-CoT system co-designed with a Graph-CoT-aware LLM serving stack (vertex-centric KV reuse + four-tier priority eviction + RetrieveNode/decode pipeline), reducing token cost by 95.7%, latency by 90.3%, and boosting throughput 15.1x while lifting Graph-CoT accuracy by up to 38%.
summary: Graph-CoT augments LLMs with iterative graph retrieval for structured multi-hop reasoning, but existing single-agent pipelines pay heavy token/latency costs (40k+ tokens, $3+ per query, 30s+ latency) while accuracy stalls below the 0.50 reliability threshold due to the lost-in-the-middle effect; serving-side, vLLM prefix caching only hits ~61%, LRU evicts to-be-reused graph facts, and vector retrieval is fully serialized. GLM jointly redesigns both layers: (1) decomposes the monolithic agent into a Classification Agent (deterministic vs. non-deterministic routing), a Reasoning Agent that operates on an incrementally-built notebook, an Action Agent that generates executable Python snippets (collapsing k1 LLM rounds into k2+1), and a Graph-RAG Retriever exposing {RetrieveNode, NodeInfo, NodeFeature, NodeDegree, neighbourCheck}; (2) introduces a Graph-CoT-aware serving stack with a vertex-centric KV reuse unit (a node plus its top-k neighbours forms a stable, nameable cache key), a four-tier priority eviction policy (shared prompt prefix P-I permanently kept, active notebook P-II, archived notebook P-III, transient missing-info P-IV, LRU within each tier), and a pipelined execution strategy that streaming-detects the RetrieveNode line in Action-Agent output to trigger asynchronous retrieval overlapping with decode, plus an LRU text->nodeID cache. On GRBench (1040 QA across 5 domains), GLM improves R-L from 0.31-0.46 to 0.55-0.77 (peak +38%), cuts per-query cost from $1.7-$3.6 to $0.1-$0.2, reduces latency from 11.3-38.6s to 2.8-5.9s, and achieves 6.8-9.1 QPS vs. 0.6-2.2 QPS (up to 15.1x).
key_findings: Multi-agent decomposition beats longer CoT: splitting classify/reason/act eliminates lost-in-the-middle, driving unexpected-agent-output errors from 41% to 2% and step-limit errors from 37% to 4%.
Code-generation Action Agent collapses k1 LLM rounds into k2+1 (avg LLM calls per query: 9-14 -> 2-3; per-call tokens: 1875-4483 -> 769-991).
Vertex chunk (node + top-k neighbours) as the KV reuse unit is the single biggest lever: +17.7% hit rate and +41.6% throughput from this alone.

LRU is a reverse signal in Graph-CoT: the most-recently-generated A-Agent suffix is the least reusable; semantic priority (shared-prefix > notebook > archived-notebook > transient) beats vLLM single-queue LRU by +14.3% throughput / -15.1% miss.
RetrieveNode almost always appears as the first line of Action-Agent snippets, enabling deterministic (not speculative) overlap with decode; pipeline alone cuts latency by 47.8%.
Accuracy gains concentrate on information-aggregation multi-hop tasks (E-commerce R-L 0.39->0.77, Healthcare 0.33->0.62) rather than single-fact lookups (Legal 0.46->0.63).
Code generation is not free lunch: code-execution errors jump from 2% to 21%, requiring a timeout + self-correction fault-tolerance loop.
limitations: Accuracy ceiling bounded by backbone LLM capacity (Qwen3-235B-A22B); requires distillation/LoRA/structure-aware prompting to decouple.
Retrieval remains dominant bottleneck on very large graphs (e.g. Legal 84M nodes) where retrieval exceeds decode window, weakening pipeline gains.
Code-execution error becomes the new dominant failure mode (48% of errors); mitigation via self-correction is heuristic, no systematic verifier.
Priority eviction and streaming RetrieveNode hook depend on non-standard vLLM v0.8.5 APIs; porting to SGLang/TRT-LLM requires rework.
C-Agent deterministic/non-deterministic classifier not quantified (no FP/FN reported); distribution shift could silently degrade.
Vertex chunk top-k neighbour truncation (by degree) may drop low-degree but query-relevant neighbours.
Notebook has no compression policy; very long multi-hop sessions may inflate Priority-II cache.

infra_impact.algorithm: Provides a reusable classify/reason/act decomposition recipe for agentic RAG; applicable to KGQA, structured information extraction, and task planning; can serve as an environment template for agentic RL (GRPO-style).
infra_impact.kernel: N/A — no new kernels; all wins come from scheduler/cache management on top of vLLM paged attention.
infra_impact.framework: Major contribution: introduces semantic priority KV eviction (replacing pure LRU) and retrieval-aware deterministic pipelining as generalizable primitives for multi-agent serving; direct relevance to vLLM / SGLang prefix-caching policy and multi-agent session APIs.
infra_impact.llm: No model modifications; multi-agent decomposition alleviates long-context lost-in-the-middle pressure, offering an alternative to raw context-window scaling.
infra_impact.agent: Formalizes the idea that agent boundaries equal KV-priority boundaries; turns multi-agent from prompt engineering into serving-system primitives; relevant to LangGraph / AutoGen / MetaGPT style orchestrators.
infra_impact.cluster: N/A — single-node (8x A800 NVLink); no networking or cross-node innovations.
infra_impact.code: ~3K Python + vLLM v0.8.5 fork; priority-eviction + streaming hook patches could become an upstream RFC to vLLM/SGLang for multi-agent session support.

open_questions: Does the RetrieveNode-first-line assumption hold across different backbones and coding styles?
Can priority tiers be learned online from runtime hit/miss logs rather than hand-engineered?
How does the approach scale when distilled Action Agents (7B/13B) replace the 235B backbone while keeping code-execution reliable?
Can vertex chunk granularity be made adaptive per query (degree vs. semantic relevance vs. PageRank)?
Can the approach integrate with SGLang RadixAttention or Mooncake-style multi-tier global KV stores?
